author: Sofia Martínez
title: Selling Honey Online: Content Strategy That Converts Local Producers
description: Local honey producers can scale beyond farmers markets. We show the content, email, and paid strategy that drives $15K+ monthly revenue for small apiaries.

sections.p: Honey is one of the easiest products to sell online, but one of the hardest to scale. Why? Because every competitor has the same story: "local," "raw," "pure," "sustainably sourced." Those words are noise. The producers who are actually scaling—the ones generating $15K-$40K monthly from ecommerce—aren't competing on story. They're competing on specificity, audience obsession, and content that teaches before it sells. We've worked with 8 apiary operators in the past 14 months. The ones with functional ecommerce revenue aren't the ones with the prettiest packaging (though that helps). They're the ones with email lists of 3,000-8,000 people, blog content ranking for long-tail searches, and a clear understanding of which customer segments are worth acquiring.

sections.h2: The Honey Customer Isn't Who You Think

sections.p: Assume your customer is someone who cares about local honey and buys a jar. That's wrong. Your actual customers are: (1) Health-conscious people searching for alternatives to commercial honey (allergies, immune support, energy, gut health), (2) Gift buyers looking for artisan/premium products ($25-60 price point), (3) Food producers (bakeries, restaurants, coffee shops) buying bulk, and (4) Niche diet followers (keto, carnivore, paleo) who use honey strategically. These are completely different audiences requiring different messaging and channels. A Kentucky apiary started segmenting their Google Ads by audience intent. One campaign targeted "raw honey benefits" and "local honey allergies" (health-focused), another targeted "artisan honey gift" and "premium honey delivery" (gift buyers), and a third targeted "bulk honey suppliers" and "restaurant honey supplier" (B2B). The health-focused campaign cost $0.68 per click with 18% conversion rate. The gift campaign cost $1.14 per click with 11% conversion rate. The B2B campaign cost $0.31 per click with 34% conversion rate. They shifted 40% of their budget to B2B overnight. That single decision increased monthly revenue 23%.

list: Create separate landing pages for each segment: one for health seekers (emphasize raw, enzyme content, research), one for gift buyers (emphasize packaging, story, premium positioning), one for B2B (emphasize bulk pricing, wholesale account terms, reliability)
Build email sequences by customer type: health buyers get content about honey and immune system; gift buyers get seasonal email campaigns; B2B gets monthly inventory/availability updates
Run separate Google Ads campaigns with keyword groups: "honey for allergies," "honey benefits" (health), "local honey gift," "artisan honey" (gifts), "raw honey bulk," "wholesale honey" (B2B)
Track which segment drives repeat customers—likely B2B, but measure it

sections.p: Most apiary sites have 4-6 product pages and a "about us" story. That's not enough to rank or to warm cold traffic. The producers scaling revenue have 25-40 pieces of pillar content covering questions their customers actually ask. Examples: "raw honey vs. pasteurized honey," "does local honey help with allergies," "how to use honey for energy," "honey keto diet," "honey for skincare," "best honey for baking," "how to store honey," "honey shelf life," "difference between clover and wildflower honey." Each piece ranks for 2-4 medium-volume keywords and functions as a conversion asset. A North Carolina apiary created a 1,200-word guide titled "Does Local Honey Help With Seasonal Allergies? What Research Actually Shows." They ranked position 4 for "local honey allergies" within 8 weeks. That single article drove 40-60 visits per month, and converted at 8.2% (3-5 sales per month from organic search alone). The article wasn't pushy—it presented research honestly, explained why local honey *might* help but wasn't a guaranteed fix, and positioned their product as "worth trying" for people interested in the theory. That honesty built trust.

Evergreen content makes sense for honey because the problem is recurring and seasonal. But seasonal content is gold. Publish articles like "best honey for holiday baking" in September, "honey skincare for winter" in October, "honey gifts for Thanksgiving" in October, and "local honey gift guide" starting August. A Wisconsin apiary published 6 seasonal guides and saw 34% of their annual ecommerce revenue concentrated in Oct-Dec because they dominated search results for gift-related queries 8 weeks before peak buying season. Their paid acquisition during Nov-Dec was pure volume play—they had the content ranking, they had the audience primed, and they just needed to remind people at decision moment.

sections.h2: Email Is Your Real Revenue Engine

sections.p: Google Ads will bring cold traffic. Email will bring repeat revenue. Every apiary we work with underestimates email ROI. A basic setup: capture emails via a lead magnet ("free guide to honey benefits" or "honey recipes"), send a welcome sequence (3 emails over 7 days introducing your story and offers), then segment lists by purchase history and send targeted campaigns. A Tennessee producer with 5,400 email subscribers calculated their email ROI at $4.2 per email sent (average). That means a 2-email campaign costs $50 in time/systems and generates $8.40 in revenue if it's just okay. If it's good (6% click-through rate, 3% conversion), it generates $32+ in revenue. Their most successful email campaign—"5 Uses for Raw Honey You Didn't Know"—had 34% open rate, 12% click rate, and 4.1% conversion (sales). One email generated $340 in revenue. The email cost them 20 minutes to write and 30 seconds to send.

list: Offer a lead magnet that actually solves a problem—not just "10% off," but a real guide or recipe ("honey recipes for energy," "honey skincare routine," "local honey immune guide")
Segment your list immediately: buyers get a post-purchase sequence; non-buyers get an education sequence; repeat buyers get VIP/seasonal access
Send weekly to buyers (they're warm), bi-weekly to engaged non-buyers, and monthly to cold/inactive (keep cost down)
Test subject lines obsessively—health-focused subject lines ("This Honey Trick Beats Store Allergy Medicine") outperform product subject lines by 2-3x open rate

sections.quote: We were spending $800/month on Facebook Ads. Our email list was 900 people getting monthly emails. We shifted that $800 to email marketing software and email-specific content creation. Within 4 months, we had 3,200 emails, email revenue was $3,200/month, and Facebook Ads was unnecessary. Email is the leverage play.

sections.h2: Paid Acquisition: When It Makes Sense

sections.p: Don't run generic "shop honey" campaigns. Run remarketing to website visitors (they've already shown interest), run search for high-intent keywords ("buy raw honey online," "bulk honey supplier," "honey delivery [city]"), and run look-alike audiences built from your email list. A Vermont apiary started with $500/month Google Ads budget. They spent the first month testing: $200 on search (health keywords), $200 on search (gift keywords), $100 on remarketing. Health keywords had 14% conversion rate, gift keywords had 7%, remarketing had 22%. They immediately shifted the budget: $100 health, $100 gift, $300 remarketing. Their ROAS improved from 1.8x to 3.1x. They stayed at $500/month but generated 73% more revenue. After 3 months of profitable testing, they increased budget to $1,200/month (still profitable at 2.8x ROAS) and hit $4,200 monthly ecommerce revenue. The key: they tested before scaling.

Facebook/Instagram is underrated for honey because audiences are segmentable by interest and behavior. Target people interested in health, wellness, sustainable living, farmers markets, beekeeping. A Michigan apiary ran a retargeting campaign to people who visited their site but didn't buy, with the creative "You Left Without Your Honey" and a 10% discount code. 3.2% of retargeting audience converted (vs. 0.6% cold audience). They spent $120 on that campaign and generated $890 in revenue. Retargeting is your highest-ROI paid channel, but only if you have warm traffic to remarket to. Build organic/content traffic first, then weaponize paid to accelerate it.
